selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: You know, I must be honest with you, I do not use social media myself. At my age, eighty-seven years, I have seen enough of the world without needing a screen to tell me what to think. I work at the restaurant here in Lecce, and every day I see young people, even some of my regular customers, glued to their phones, reading things that worry them or make them angry. They show me sometimes, asking what I think, and frankly, much of what I see seems designed to upset people rather than inform them. From what I observe in others, this fake content problem is very serious. People believe things without questioning, without asking where the information comes from. In my younger days, we had newspapers, and yes, sometimes they were biased, but at least you knew who was speaking. Now anyone can say anything, and it spreads like wildfire. My advice, though I admit I am speaking from the outside looking in, is to be very careful about what you trust. Talk to real people, face to face. When customers at my tables discuss news or current events, I always encourage them to verify things through multiple sources, not just what appears on their phone screens. I think about my own faith, Islam, and how easily misinformation spreads about religious communities. People see one false story and suddenly they believe terrible things about their neighbors. It troubles me deeply. The solution, I believe, is old-fashioned skepticism and human connection. Before you share something or believe it completely, ask yourself if it makes sense, if it comes from someone trustworthy. Better yet, put the phone down and have a real conversation. Life is difficult enough without filling our minds with lies and distortions. I may not understand all the technology, but I understand people, and I know that we must be more careful, more thoughtful, and more willing to question what we are told. The truth still matters, even if finding it requires more effort than it once did.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is an 87-year-old man who explicitly has no social media presence, which is central to his perspective. His high extraversion and agreeableness suggest he values human connection and face-to-face interaction. His high conscientiousness means he would value truth and verification. His high neuroticism and depressed state would make him concerned about the negative impacts of misinformation. His age and traditional occupation as a waiter give him an observational perspective rather than direct experience. His Muslim faith in Italy makes him aware of how misinformation can affect communities. His moderate openness means he's somewhat traditional in his views. The tone should be warm but concerned, conversational yet thoughtful, reflecting someone who observes social media's effects on others rather than experiencing it directly.
response_parsed.tone: concerned, observational, conversational, grandfatherly, somewhat worried but kind
